title: Failure, Teaching, and the Skill Stack
thesis: Building substantive skills requires tolerating bad early work and using teaching as a forcing function for clarity; the resulting skill stack compounds even when individual projects fail.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Early work is supposed to be bad; the gap between taste and ability closes through repetition and feedback.

evidence.snippet: Deliberate practice—repeated performance of intended behavior with feedback and correction—accounts for much of the variance in the acquisition of expert performance.
Ira Glass describes the gap between a beginner's taste and their ability, and argues that the only way to close it is to produce a large volume of work.

counterevidence.summary: Quality instruction, prior knowledge, and supportive environments can reduce the amount of unproductive early failure; repetition without feedback can entrench bad habits.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Teaching forces you to clarify what you actually understand.

evidence.snippet: Elaborative interrogation and self-explanation are high-utility learning techniques because they require learners to generate explanations and identify gaps in understanding.
Learning by teaching produces meaningful gains because preparing to teach encourages learners to organize knowledge and monitor their own comprehension.

counterevidence.summary: Teaching can reinforce misconceptions if the teacher's own understanding is shaky; it works best when paired with some baseline comprehension and corrective feedback.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: A portfolio of unfinished but documented projects is more valuable than a hidden record of perfect beginnings.

evidence.snippet: The taste-ability gap can only be closed by producing a large volume of work, most of which will be imperfect at the start.
Expert performance develops through sustained effort in a domain, with accumulated experience providing the foundation for increasingly complex skills.

counterevidence.summary: In fields where credentials or polished deliverables gate opportunity, visible unfinished work may be penalized; documentation is not a substitute for meeting entry requirements.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: AI can accelerate feedback loops, but it cannot replace the judgment that comes from repeated practice.

evidence.snippet: Active learning techniques such as practice testing and distributed practice outperform passive review because they require the learner to generate and evaluate responses.
The benefits of learning by teaching stem from generative processing and metacognitive monitoring, which cannot be fully delegated to an external tool.

counterevidence.summary: Adaptive AI tutoring systems have demonstrated learning gains in some settings; whether AI substitutes for or scaffolds judgment depends on how learners use it, not on the tool alone.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: Ericsson: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance
Ira Glass on the gap between taste and ability
Dunlosky et al.: Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques
Fiorella & Mayer: The Cambridge Handbook of Multimedia Learning — Learning by Teaching

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and keep each claim distinct from adjacent articles in the series.
Preserve the plain-language, encouraging-but-realistic tone; do not promise rapid mastery or frame failure as a final outcome.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not generalize beyond the public evidence.
Treat AI as a scaffold for practice, not a replacement for the judgment that comes from repeated attempts and feedback.
Flag when a recommendation is a practical heuristic rather than an empirically tested intervention.
Article includes a skill-stack loop Mermaid diagram in 'The Documented Skill Stack' section; when discussing the skill stack, reference the cycle of early work, teaching, feedback, and reuse.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 fold of the-small-rep-theory and the series migration (issue #124 instruction).
